one_line_takeaway: STRAP-ViT detects ViT tokens that statistically diverge (via sqrt-JSD) from a clean reference and neutralizes only those tokens with randomized composite transforms, recovering ImageNet Top-1 accuracy from single digits to within a few points of clean baseline against several adversarial-patch attacks, without retraining.
key_finding: On ViT-base-16/ImageNet, STRAP-ViT raises Top-1 accuracy from 3.6% (undefended) to 78.8% under a 40x40 Adversarial Patch, from 7.4% to 79.2% under 40x40 LAVAN, and from 31.9% to 77.2% under 40x40 GDPA, versus an 80.5% clean baseline (STRAP-ViT-on-clean: 80.1%, a 0.4-point drop). Robustness holds across patch sizes up to 50x50 and generalizes to DinoV2 (e.g., 7.2% -> 83.9% Top-1 on ImageNet under 40x40 Adversarial Patch, clean baseline 83.8%). Against the stronger multi-patch RP2 attack with K raised to 4-8, Top-1 recovers from 12.9%/3.1% (undefended, 4/5 patches) to 76.1%/73.4%. In the head-to-head comparison (Table IV, ViT/ImageNet/40x40 Adversarial Patch), STRAP-ViT reports 78.8% robust accuracy versus 74.3% (ODDR), 70.6% (JEDI and LGS, tied), 62.6% (Dimension Reduction), 62.1% (PatchCleanser), 41.7% (Certifiable Defense with ViT), and 39% (Smoothed ViT-B) — the highest of the compared methods under this setting. Under the paper's own adaptive-attack attempt (JSD-constrained patch optimization jointly accounting for all three transforms), robust accuracy drops by 'not more than 15%' relative to the clean baseline, which the authors interpret as evidence the transform randomization meaningfully raises the bar for adaptive attackers.
core_contribution: STRAP-ViT is a training-free, plug-and-play defense against physically-realizable adversarial patches on Vision Transformers, inserted right after the patch-embedding layer. It has two stages: (1) Detection through Token Segregation — for each token at a given layer, compute a probability vector over channels via temperature softmax, compare it against a position/layer-conditional clean reference distribution (the mean of clean-sample token distributions) using the square root of Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD), and flag tokens whose score exceeds a threshold calibrated on clean data at a chosen false-alarm level; (2) Mitigation through Token Transformation — apply a randomly chosen composition (random non-empty subset and order) of up to three transforms — an Lp-norm projection, a bounded-operator-norm affine contraction, and a temperature-scaled softmax ('simplex') transform — only to the flagged tokens, leaving all other tokens untouched, before continuing the normal ViT forward pass. A hyperparameter K (minimum number of tokens to transform, tuned so at least 50% of a patch's footprint is covered) governs the detection-to-mitigation cutoff; the paper explores K = 2 (~1% of tokens), 4 (~2%), and 8 (~4%).
threat_model: White-box: the attacker has full knowledge of the victim ViT's architecture and parameters and can substitute a bounded region (paper cites <=5% of the image) with an adversarial patch optimized (via an Expectation-Over-Transformation / PGD objective) to force a target misclassification. Assumed patch properties are localization (small, bounded region), transferability across images/models, and physical realizability (printable, robust to lighting/viewpoint changes). The paper also explicitly analyzes an adaptive attacker in Section V who has full knowledge of STRAP-ViT itself and tries to (a) craft patches whose JSD score stays below the highest JSD of any clean-image fragment, and (b) train the patch to survive all three composite transforms simultaneously (an EOT-style adaptation).
limitations: The paper's own reported numbers contain at least one internal inconsistency worth flagging: in Table II (DinoV2), 'STRAP-ViT on Clean' ImageNet Top-1 is listed as 86.0% (+2.2% relative to an 83.8% clean baseline) even though the corresponding Top-5 for the same row drops (97.3%, -2.0%) and every other clean-sample-degradation row in Tables I-III shows a decrease -- a defense mechanism improving clean Top-1 accuracy while degrading Top-5 on the same setting is not explained anywhere in the text. The same Table II also reports, for DinoV2/ImageNet/Adversarial-Patch 45x45, a STRAP-ViT Top-5 accuracy of 100.3% (+81.8%) -- a value above 100% that is logically impossible for an accuracy metric and stronger evidence of a data/typo error in this table than the +2.2% anomaly above. Table III's RP2 patch-count rows (TWO/THREE/FOUR patches with K=4, K=4, K=8) reuse numbers identical to Table I's single-patch GDPA rows (40x40/45x45/50x50), which is either a genuine coincidence in experimental design (RP2 configured to match GDPA's per-row severity) or a copy artifact; the paper does not comment on this overlap. The adaptive-attack analysis in Section V is described only qualitatively ('generates patches ... limited adversarial attack success rate ... not more than 15%' drop) without a table, exact numbers, or attack budget/iteration details, so its rigor cannot be independently assessed from the text. The clean-reference distribution q_{l,t} is defined as an expectation over a set of clean images C, but the paper never specifies the size or composition of C or how sensitive detection is to this reference set. Evaluation is limited to classification (no detection/segmentation tasks), two datasets, and image classifiers only; the authors explicitly flag extension to (L)VLMs as future work rather than demonstrated. No formal/certified robustness guarantee is provided -- this is an empirical defense, and Table IV's baseline numbers are taken from other papers' reported results rather than the authors' own re-implementation under identical conditions, so cross-paper comparability of experimental settings (compute, exact attack hyperparameters) is not fully controlled.

quantitative_results: ViT-B/16, ImageNet, clean baseline: 80.5% Top-1 / 95.4% Top-5
ViT-B/16, ImageNet, STRAP-ViT on clean: 80.1% Top-1 (-0.4%) / 95.1% Top-5 (-0.3%)
ViT-B/16, ImageNet, Adversarial Patch 40x40: no-defence 3.6% Top-1 -> STRAP-ViT 78.8% Top-1 (+75.2 pts); 16.4% -> 94.2% Top-5
ViT-B/16, ImageNet, LAVAN 40x40: no-defence 7.4% Top-1 -> STRAP-ViT 79.2% Top-1 (+71.8 pts)
ViT-B/16, ImageNet, GDPA 40x40: no-defence 31.9% Top-1 -> STRAP-ViT 77.2% Top-1 (+45.3 pts)
DinoV2, ImageNet, clean baseline: 83.8% Top-1 / 99.3% Top-5
DinoV2, ImageNet, Adversarial Patch 40x40: no-defence 7.2% Top-1 -> STRAP-ViT 83.9% Top-1 (+76.7 pts)
ViT-B/16, ImageNet, RP2 4-patch (30x30 each), K=8: no-defence 12.9% Top-1 -> STRAP-ViT 76.1% Top-1
ViT-B/16, ImageNet, RP2 5-patch (25x25 each), K=8: no-defence 3.1% Top-1 -> STRAP-ViT 73.4% Top-1

Table IV comparison (ViT, ImageNet, Adversarial Patch, robust accuracy): at 40x40 -- STRAP-ViT 78.8% vs ODDR 74.3%, JEDI 70.6%, LGS 70.6%, Dimension Reduction 62.6%; at 32x32 -- PatchCleanser 62.1%, Certifiable Defense with ViT 41.7%, Smoothed ViT-B 39% (patch size not held constant across the table)
Adaptive attack (paper's own attempt against STRAP-ViT): accuracy drop of 'not more than 15%' relative to clean baseline
baselines_compared: Certifiable Defense with ViT (Chen et al.)
LGS - Local Gradients Smoothing (Naseer et al.)
ODDR - Outlier Detection & Dimension Reduction (Chattopadhyay et al.)
